mainTopic: (continues) סימנים בגט · מצא גט אישה בשוק · מצא שטר שחרור בשוק
overview: (1) Rabbi Yirmiya and Rav Ashi discuss the validity of signs (סימנים) on a document, such as witnesses testifying to a unique name or a specific hole, and Rav bar bar Chanah's uncertainty about the legal status of such signs. (2) The Gemara analyzes the Mishnah regarding a found get when the husband admits, questioning the risk of backdating and the wife collecting from buyers, and explains that the wife must prove when she received the get. (3) The Gemara discusses a found emancipation document, raising the same issue of backdating and the master selling the slave's property, and resolves it by requiring the slave to prove when he received the document. (4) The Gemara defines 'deyateika' (wills) and 'matana' (gifts), discussing the acquisition of property through 'from today and after death' clauses, and raises a contradiction regarding returning found wills and gifts.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: Rabbi Yirmiya and Rav Ashi discuss the validity of signs (סימנים) on a document, such as witnesses testifying to a unique name or a specific hole, and Rav bar bar Chanah's uncertainty about the legal status of such signs.

The Gemara analyzes the Mishnah regarding a found get when the husband admits, questioning the risk of backdating and the wife collecting from buyers, and explains that the wife must prove when she received the get.

The Gemara discusses a found emancipation document, raising the same issue of backdating and the master selling the slave's property, and resolves it by requiring the slave to prove when he received the document.

The Gemara defines 'deyateika' (wills) and 'matana' (gifts), discussing the acquisition of property through 'from today and after death' clauses, and raises a contradiction regarding returning found wills and gifts.

steps.title: The Mishnah's ruling on a found גט
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces the Mishnah's ruling that if a husband admits to writing a גט, the finder returns it to the wife, but if he does not admit, it is returned to neither party.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces the source text (Mishnah) that will be analyzed. This is a setup line returning to the main topic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is the foundational ruling. The גמרא will now probe implications of returning the גט when the husband admits, focusing on potential financial harm to third parties. It sets the stage for the next challenge.

keyTerms.meaning: He returns it to the wife

phrases.english: The matter itself: One found a woman's bill of divorce in the marketplace,
when the husband admits — he returns it to the wife.
The husband does not admit —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: he does not return it, neither to this one nor to that one.

steps.triggerLanguage: גּוּפָא: מָצָא גֵּט אִשָּׁה בַּשּׁוּק, בִּזְמַן שֶׁהַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה – יַחְזִיר לְאִשָּׁה. אֵין הַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה – לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.

steps.title: Challenge: The risk of backdating and collecting from buyers
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the Mishnah's ruling by raising the possibility that the גט was written in Nisan but given in Tishrei, allowing the wife to unlawfully collect property sold by the husband in the interim.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the Mishnah's ruling to return the גט when the husband admits. This is a direct challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The challenge assumes the גט might be backdated. If the husband sold property between writing and giving, the wife could use the גט to reclaim that property from buyers, causing them a loss. This suggests the Mishnah's ruling is problematic.

keyTerms.meaning: Did not give it to her until Tishrei
To repossess from the buyers unlawfully

phrases.aramaic: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה מִיהָא יַחְזִיר לָאִשָּׁה.
phrases.english: At the time that the husband admits, from here he returns to the woman.

phrases.aramaic: וְלֵיחוּשׁ שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִיתֵּן בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא
phrases.english: And let us suspect perhaps he wrote to give in Nisan and not
gave to her until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲזַל בַּעַל זַבֵּין פֵּירֵי מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי,
phrases.english: and the husband went and sold produce from Nisan and until Tishrei,
and she brings out a bill of divorce written in Nisan,
and comes to repossess the purchases not by law.

steps.triggerLanguage: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה מִיהָא יַחְזִיר לָאִשָּׁה. וְלֵיחוּשׁ שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִיתֵּן בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא נָתַן לָהּ עַד תִּשְׁרֵי, וַאֲזַל בַּעַל זַבֵּין פֵּירֵי מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי, וּמַפְּקָא לְגִטָּא דִּכְתִיב בְּנִיסָן, וְאָתְיָא לְמִטְרַף לָקוֹחוֹת שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין.

rashi.hebrew: ולא נתן עד תשרי - וכל זמן שלא נתן היה לו לאכול ולמכור פירי נכסי מלוג שלה שתקנו חכמים לבעל לאכול פירות של נכסי מלוג אשתו שנפלו לה לירושה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the husband's right to sell the fruits of the wife's נכסי מלוג between the writing and giving of the גט is based on the חכמים's enactment granting him this right.
רש״י clarifies that the husband sold the fruits in accordance with his legal right to do so.

tosafot.hebrew: וליחוש דלמא כתב ליתן בניסן כו' - ואפילו לאביי דאמר עדיו בחתומיו זכין לו הכא גבי אשה חוב הוא לה ואמתני' דדייקינן הא אמר תנו נותנין וכן אמתני' דהמביא גט ממדינת הים לא פריך ניחוש שמא כתב ליתן בניסן כו' כיון דעתה שבא לגרשה רואין שהזמן מוקדם ויש קול שלא נמסר ביום שנכתב ויזכרו לקוחות לומר אייתי ראיה אימת מטא גיטא לידך אבל הכא שהבעל מודה שממנה נפל וגירשה ביום הכתיבה פריך וניחוש שמא יסברו לקוחות שאמרה אמת ולא יאמרו בשעת טריפה אייתי ראייה אימת מטא גיטא לידך וא"ת ואמאי לא פריך אמתני' דכותבין גט לאיש אע"פ שאין אשתו עמו ליחוש שמא לא יתן עד שנה וכי תימא כיון דליכא ריעותא דנפילה י"ל יום שנכתב נמסר ואפי' נמסר נמי אחר זמן יש קול שלא נמסר ביום שנכתב כי רואים בשעת קבלת הגט שהוא מוקדם ויזכרו לקוחות לומר כשתטרף אייתי ראיה דהא לעיל (בבא מציעא דף יב:) פריך גבי כותבין שטר ללוה ויש לומר דגבי כותבין שטר ללוה פריך שפיר משום דמאן דיזיף בצנעא יזיף (ב"ב מב.) ויש לחוש שמא לאחר זמן ימסור לו בצנעא ויסברו לקוחות שנמסר לו משעת כתיבה אבל גט דיהבי בפרהסיא דצריך עדי מסירה אפילו לר"מ דאין דבר שבערוה פחות משנים כדפירש ר"ת ורשב"ם בגיטין (ד. ד"ה דק"ל) ועוד דרגילות לגרש בפרהסיא לגלות שהיא פנויה יש קול שנתגרשה אחר זמן כתיבה ולא יטעו הלקוחות אבל אכתי קשה אמאי כותבין גט לאיש כו' ניחוש שמא תזנה אחר הכתיבה ויתן לה בצנעא כדי לחפות עליה וי"ל דמיד שנכתב הגט נותן לה דלא מקדים איניש פורענות לנפשיה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara's concern about backdating applies here because the husband admits the גט fell from her hand, leading buyers to believe it was given on the writing date, unlike other cases where public knowledge or the nature of the transaction prevents such confusion.

steps.title: Resolution: Distinguishing between two opinions on פירות
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by distinguishing between the opinion that the husband loses rights to פירות upon deciding to divorce and the opinion that he retains them until the גט is given.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a resolution to the challenge by appealing to a dispute regarding the husband's rights to the wife's פירות. This is a direct resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: According to the opinion that the husband loses rights to פירות once he decides to divorce, the challenge is moot because he had no right to sell the produce. The difficulty only remains for the opinion that he retains rights until the גט is given. It resolves the tension by splitting the case.

keyTerms.meaning: Once he has decided to divorce her
The husband no longer has rights to the produce

keyTerms.term: יֵשׁ לַבַּעַל פֵּירוֹת עַד שְׁעַת נְתִינָה
keyTerms.meaning: The husband has rights to the produce until the time of giving

phrases.aramaic: הָנִיחָא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר כֵּיוָן שֶׁנָּתַן עֵינָיו
phrases.english: It is fine for the one who says: once he set his eyes

phrases.aramaic: לְגָרְשָׁהּ שׁוּב אֵין לַבַּעַל פֵּירוֹת – שַׁפִּיר.
phrases.english: to divorce her, the husband no longer has produce — fine.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר יֵשׁ לַבַּעַל פֵּירוֹת
phrases.english: But for the one who says: the husband has produce
until the time of giving,
what is there to say?

steps.triggerLanguage: הָנִיחָא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר כֵּיוָן שֶׁנָּתַן עֵינָיו לְגָרְשָׁהּ שׁוּב אֵין לַבַּעַל פֵּירוֹת – שַׁפִּיר. אֶלָּא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר יֵשׁ לַבַּעַל פֵּירוֹת עַד שְׁעַת נְתִינָה, מַאי אִיכָּא לְמֵימַר?

rashi.hebrew: שפיר - יש לה לטרוף כל מה שמכר משעת כתיבה והא פלוגתא במסכת גיטין בפ' שני (דף יז:):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to the opinion that the husband loses rights to פירות upon deciding to divorce, the wife can retract all sales he made from the time of writing the גט, as this dispute is discussed in מסכת גיטין.

steps.title: Resolution: The wife must prove when she received the גט
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara answers that if the wife comes to repossess property, she must first prove when the גט came into her possession.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara resolves the challenge by placing the burden of proof on the wife. She must demonstrate when the גט reached her possession before she can reclaim property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The court will not allow her to use the גט to collect property immediately. Instead, she must provide evidence that the גט was delivered before the property was sold to third parties.

keyTerms.meaning: When the גט came into your possession

steps.whatToRemember: The wife must provide proof of when she received the גט to prevent unlawful collection.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'טרף' refers to repossessing property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a procedural safeguard to protect third parties from financial loss.

phrases.english: When she comes to repossess, we say to her:

phrases.aramaic: אַיְיתַי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא גִּיטָּא לְיָדְךָ.
phrases.english: Bring proof when the bill of divorce came into your hand.

steps.triggerLanguage: כִּי אָתְיָא לְמִטְרַף אָמְרִינַן לַהּ: אַיְיתַי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא גִּיטָּא לְיָדְךָ.

rashi.hebrew: אימת מטא גיטא לידך - מיד בעליך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the question אימת מטא גיטא לידך specifically asks when the גט reached her from her former husband, establishing the timeline of the divorce.

tosafot.hebrew: אימת מטא גיטא לידך - בשאר גיטין לא אמרינן כן דמסתמא ביום שנכתב נמסר אבל זה דנפל אתרע:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that this requirement to prove the timing of the גט's delivery is unique to this case because the גט fell into a pit (נפל אתרע), whereas normally we assume a גט is delivered on the day it was written.

steps.title: Challenge: Why is a גט different from שטר חוב?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the resolution by asking why the same logic does not apply to שטר חוב, where the Mishnah rules not to return the document even if the debtor admits.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a difficulty against the resolution by comparing the case of a גט to that of a שטר חוב.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara notes that in the case of a שטר חוב, the Mishnah rules not to return the document even if the debtor admits, precisely due to the risk of backdating and unlawful collection. If the resolution for the גט is to require proof of when it was given, why not apply the same logic to the שטר חוב and return it with the same condition?

keyTerms.meaning: What is different about promissory notes
If they include a property guarantee

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge highlights an apparent inconsistency between the rulings on גט and שטר חוב.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'שטר חוב' refers to a promissory note or debt document.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the Gemara to distinguish between the two types of documents.

phrases.english: And what is the difference from promissory notes?
For we learned: One who found promissory notes,
If they contain a property guarantee –
He shall not return them. And we established it as when the debtor admits,

phrases.aramaic: וּמִשּׁוּם שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִלְווֹת בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא
phrases.english: And because perhaps he wrote to borrow in Nisan and did not

phrases.aramaic: לָוָה עַד תִּשְׁרֵי וְקָא טָרֵיף לָקוֹחוֹת שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין.
phrases.english: Borrow until Tishrei and is seizing the purchasers' property not according to law.

steps.triggerLanguage: וּמַאי שְׁנָא מִשְּׁטָרֵי חוֹב? דִּתְנַן: מָצָא שְׁטָרֵי חוֹב, אִם יֵשׁ בָּהֶן אַחְרָיוּת נְכָסִים – לֹא יַחְזִיר. וְאוֹקֵימְנָא כְּשֶׁחַיָּיב מוֹדֶה, וּמִשּׁוּם שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִלְווֹת בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא לָוָה עַד תִּשְׁרֵי וְקָא טָרֵיף לָקוֹחוֹת שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין.

steps.title: Resolution: The same logic applies to שטר חוב
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara initially suggests that the same logic should apply to שטר חוב, and the creditor should be required to prove when the document came into his possession.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara proposes that the resolution for the גט should also apply to the שטר חוב.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a temporary resolution that sets up the next step, where the Gemara will explain why this is not the case. It highlights the apparent symmetry between the two cases.

keyTerms.meaning: There too it should be returned

steps.whatToRemember: The Gemara initially sees no difference between the two cases.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a rhetorical step that leads to the actual resolution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the Gemara to articulate the distinction between the two cases.

phrases.english: There too it should be returned,
And when he comes to seize [property], let them say to him:

phrases.aramaic: אַיְיתִי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא שְׁטַר חוֹב לְיָדָךְ!
phrases.english: Bring proof when the debt document came into your hand!

steps.triggerLanguage: הָתָם נָמֵי לִיהְדַּר, וְכִי אָתֵי לְמִטְרַף נֵימָא לֵיהּ: אַיְיתִי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא שְׁטַר חוֹב לְיָדָךְ!

rashi.hebrew: אימת מטא שטרא לידך - מיד הלוה מתחלה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the question אימת מטא שטרא לידך refers to when the שטר חוב first came into the creditor's possession from the borrower.

steps.title: Resolution: The distinction based on the buyer's perspective
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that the distinction lies in the buyer's perspective: in the case of a גט, the buyer will demand proof from the wife, but in the case of a שטר חוב, the buyer will not demand proof from the creditor.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides the actual resolution by distinguishing between the two cases based on the buyer's perspective.
steps.deeperAnalysis: In the case of a גט, the buyer will argue that the Sages only returned the גט to prevent the wife from being an עגונה, not to allow her to collect property. Therefore, she must prove when she received it. In the case of a שטר חוב, the buyer will not make this argument because the Sages returned the document to allow the creditor to collect, implying that the document is valid from the date written on it.

keyTerms.meaning: Here, with regard to a woman's גט
The buyer comes and demands from her
So that she would not be an עגונה

steps.whatToRemember: The distinction is based on the purpose for which the Sages returned the document.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'עגונה' refers to a woman who is unable to remarry due to lack of a גט.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows how the Gemara uses the underlying purpose of a ruling to distinguish between similar cases.

phrases.aramaic: אָמְרִי, הָכָא גַּבֵּי גֵּט אִשָּׁה אָתֵי לוֹקֵחַ וְתָבַעה,
phrases.english: They say: Here, with regard to a woman's bill of divorce, a purchaser comes and demands proof,

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר: הַאי דְּ[אַ]הְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלַהּ רַבָּנַן לְגִיטָּא,
phrases.english: He says: The fact that the Sages returned it to her was only so that…
Now that she is coming to repossess property –
Let her go and bring proof as to when the bill of divorce came into…

phrases.aramaic: תֵּיזִל וְתַיְתֵי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא גִּיטָּא לִידַהּ.
phrases.english: she should go and bring proof as to when the bill of divorce came into her possession.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמְרִי, הָכָא גַּבֵּי גֵּט אִשָּׁה אָתֵי לוֹקֵחַ וְתָבַעה, אָמַר: הַאי דְּ[אַ]הְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלַהּ רַבָּנַן לְגִטָּא, מִשּׁוּם דְּלָא תִּעֲגִין וְתֵיתִיב. הַשְּׁתָּא דְּקָא אָתְיָא לְמִטְרַף – תֵּיזִל וְתַיְתֵי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא גִּיטָּא לִידַהּ.

rashi.hebrew: אתי לוקח ותבע - אי אתיא למיטרף מסיק אדעתיה לתבוע אייתי ראיה אימת מטא גיטא לידך בזמנא קמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the buyer's argument is that the Sages only returned the גט to prevent the woman from being an עגונה, so she must bring proof of when the גט actually reached her hand.

steps.title: Resolution: The buyer's inference in the case of שטר חוב
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that in the case of a שטר חוב, the buyer will not demand proof because he infers from the Sages' ruling that the document is valid from the date written on it.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara completes the resolution by explaining the buyer's inference in the case of a שטר חוב.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The buyer will reason that the Sages only returned the שטר חוב to allow the creditor to collect property, which implies that the document is valid from the date written on it. Therefore, the buyer will not demand proof of when the creditor received the document, as he assumes it was received before the property was sold.

keyTerms.meaning: Here, with regard to a promissory note
The buyer does not come and demand
From the fact that the Sages returned it to him

steps.whatToRemember: The buyer's inference is based on the purpose for which the Sages returned the document.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'משמע מינה' refers to inferring from the ruling.
steps.whyThisMatters: This completes the distinction between the two cases and resolves the challenge.

phrases.aramaic: הָכָא גַּבֵּי שְׁטַר חוֹב לָא אָתֵי לוֹקֵחַ וְתָבַע,
phrases.english: Here, with regard to a promissory note, the purchaser will not come and demand
[Says] Because the Sages returned it to him, the promissory note
Obviously, for what halakha did they return it to him?

phrases.aramaic: לְמִטְרַף הוּא, שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ קָמוּ רַבָּנַן בְּמִילְּתָא,
phrases.english: To repossess with it, from there they heard that the Sages stood in the matter
And before mine, the document reached him.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָכָא גַּבֵּי שְׁטַר חוֹב לָא אָתֵי לוֹקֵחַ וְתָבַע, [אָמַר] מִדְּאַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ רַבָּנַן לִשְׁטַר חוֹב, פְּשִׁיטָא לְמַאי הִלְכְתָא אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ – לְמִטְרַף הוּא, שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ קָמוּ רַבָּנַן בְּמִילְּתָא, וּמִקַּמֵּי דִּידִי מְטָא שְׁטָרָא לִידֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: מקמי דידי - מקודם שמכר לי הלוה נכסיו מטא שטר מיד לוה למלוה בראשונה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase מקמי דידי means that the שטר חוב was delivered to the creditor before the debtor sold the property to the buyer.

steps.title: The baraita on finding a bill of manumission
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a baraita that rules on the case of finding a bill of manumission in the marketplace, distinguishing between when the master admits to giving it and when he does not.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a baraita that establishes the basic halachic framework for a found bill of manumission.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The baraita sets up a dichotomy: if the master admits he gave the document to the slave, it is returned to the slave; if he does not admit, it is not returned to either party. This establishes the baseline rule that the Gemara will now challenge.
steps.whatToRemember: The baraita's ruling depends entirely on the master's admission.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'רב' here refers to the master of the slave, not a rabbi.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the core tension: why does the master's admission matter if the document itself seems to prove the slave's freedom?

phrases.english: Freed slaves, etc. The Sages taught:
If one found a bill of manumission in the marketplace,
when the master admits — he returns it to the slave,
the master does not admit —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: he does not return it neither to this one nor to that one.

steps.triggerLanguage: תנו רבנן: מצא שטר שחרור בשוק

steps.title: Challenge: The danger of backdating and lost sales
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the baraita's ruling to return the document when the master admits, raising the concern that the document might be backdated, allowing the slave to reclaim property the master sold while the slave was still enslaved.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara attacks the baraita's leniency by constructing a scenario where returning the document causes financial loss to third-party buyers.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara posits a timeline: the master wrote the document in Nisan but gave it in Tishrei. In the interim, the slave bought property (which legally belonged to the master). The master sold that property. If the document is returned, the slave can use its Nisan date to reclaim the property from the buyers, causing them a loss. This challenges the baraita's permission to return the document.
steps.whatToRemember: The core issue is the potential for the document to be used to reclaim property sold by the master during the period between writing and delivery.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'טרף' here means to repossess or claw back property from buyers.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces the concept of 'ניחוש' (let us suspect) as a tool to challenge lenient rulings based on potential future harm to third parties.

phrases.aramaic: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהָרַב מוֹדֶה מִיהָא יַחְזִיר לָעֶבֶד, וְאַמַּאי?
phrases.english: At the time that the master admits, at least he will return to the slave, and why?

phrases.aramaic: נֵיחוּשׁ שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִיתֵּן לוֹ בְּנִיסָן
phrases.english: Let us suspect perhaps he wrote to give to him in Nisan
and he did not give to him until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲזַל עַבְדָּא וּקְנָה נִכְסִין מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי,
phrases.english: and the slave went and acquired property from Nisan and until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲזַיל הָרַב וְזַבְּנִינְהוּ וּמַפֵּיק לֵיהּ לְשִׁחְרוּר דִּכְתִב בְּנִיסָן,
phrases.english: and the master goes and sells them and produces for him the manumission written in Nisan,
and he is repossessing the purchasers not according to law.

steps.triggerLanguage: בזמן שהרב מודה מיהא יחזיר לעבד, ואמאי?

steps.title: Question: The difficulty according to the view that freedom is a burden
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara notes that the challenge works well according to the view that freedom is a benefit and the document takes effect upon signing, but asks how to resolve the case according to the view that freedom is a burden.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara narrows the scope of the challenge by identifying which halachic opinions are affected by the difficulty.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara distinguishes between two views on the nature of emancipation: one where it is a benefit (זכות) and takes effect upon signing (as per Abaye), and one where it is a burden (חוב) and requires delivery. The challenge of backdating only applies if the document takes effect upon signing. If it requires delivery, the Nisan date is irrelevant, and the challenge falls away. The Gemara asks how the baraita's ruling works according to the latter view.

keyTerms.meaning: its witnesses acquire it for him upon signing

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge only applies if the document takes effect upon signing.
steps.confusionAlert: The terms 'זכות' and 'חוב' here refer to whether emancipation is considered a benefit or a burden to the slave, which determines when the document takes legal effect.
steps.whyThisMatters: This highlights the interplay between the nature of the transaction (benefit vs. burden) and the timing of its legal effect.

phrases.aramaic: הָנִיחָא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר זְכוּת הוּא לָעֶבֶד
phrases.english: It is fine for the one who says it is a benefit for the slave

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁיּוֹצֵא מִתַּחַת רַבּוֹ לְחֵירוּת, וּכְאַבָּיֵי דְּאָמַר:
phrases.english: that he goes out from under his master to freedom, and according to Abaye who…
his witnesses with their signatures benefit him, it is fine.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא לְמַאן דְּאָמַר חוֹב הוּא לָעֶבֶד
phrases.english: But for the one who says it is a burden for the slave
that he goes out from under his master to freedom,
what is there to say?

steps.triggerLanguage: הניחא למה דאמר זכות הוא לעבד

rashi.hebrew: הניחא למ"ד כו' - פלוגתא בפ"ק דגיטין (דף יב:):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the dispute regarding whether emancipation is a benefit or a burden is found in the first chapter of גיטין.

rashi.hebrew: זכות הוא לעבד - טובה היא אצלו שמתירו בקהל וכיון דזכות הוא איכא לתרוצי דמשעה שנכתב זוכה בשחרור דלכי מטא שטר גט לידיה נעשה בן חורין למפרע משעת חתימה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to the view that emancipation is a benefit (זכות), the slave acquires his freedom retroactively from the time of signing, resolving the difficulty.

rashi.hebrew: חוב הוא לעבד - חובתו הוא שיוצא מתחת רבו כלומר הפסד הוא לו שאם עבד כהן הוא פוסלו מן התרומה ואם של ישראל אוסרו בשפחה כנענית דזילא ליה ושכיחא ליה ופריצא ליה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the view considering emancipation a burden (חוב) stems from the loss of privileges, such as a כהן losing the right to תרומה or a ישראל being forbidden from a כנענית.

rashi.hebrew: מאי איכא למימר - דהשתא ליכא למימר עדיו בחתומיו חבין לו דאין חבין לאדם שלא בפניו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the difficulty arises because one cannot effect a burden (חוב) upon a person without their presence, making the signing alone insufficient.

steps.title: Resolution: Requiring proof of delivery
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by stating that the slave must bring proof of when he received the document to establish when his freedom took effect.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the contradiction by requiring the slave to prove when he received the שטר שחרור. This setup allows the baraita's ruling to stand even if freedom is considered a burden.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The burden of proof shifts to the slave. He must demonstrate the exact date the document reached his possession. If he received it in תשרי, he cannot reclaim property sold in ניסן, protecting the buyers.
steps.whatToRemember: The recipient must prove the date of delivery to establish the date of emancipation.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'רְאָיָה' here means proof or evidence.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a principle that when a document's effect depends on delivery, the recipient must prove when delivery occurred to claim rights based on it.

phrases.english: When he comes to repossess, we say to him:

phrases.aramaic: אַיְיתִי רְאָיָה אֵימַת מְטָא שִׁחְרוּר לְיָדָךְ.
phrases.english: Bring proof as to when the bill of manumission reached your hand.

steps.title: Definition of דְּיָיתֵיקֵי and מַתָּנָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a ברייתא defining דְּיָיתֵיקֵי as a document stating 'this will stand if I die' and מַתָּנָה as one stating 'from today and after death'.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a ברייתא that defines the two types of documents mentioned in the משנה: דְּיָיתֵיקֵי and מַתָּנָה. This is a setup line clarifying terminology.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא clarifies the terminology. דְּיָיתֵיקֵי is defined as a document that takes effect only upon death. מַתָּנָה is defined as a document containing the phrase 'מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה'. This sets up the legal distinction between a standard will and a gift document.

keyTerms.meaning: A type of will or deed

steps.whatToRemember: The definition of מַתָּנָה here is specific to the phrase 'מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה'.
steps.confusionAlert: Do not confuse this definition with the general concept of a gift; here it refers specifically to the wording in the document.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding when the recipient acquires the property.

phrases.english: Deyaytiki, a gift, etc. The Sages taught:
What is it, deyaytiki –

phrases.aramaic: דָּא תְּהֵא לְמֵיקַם וְלִהְיוֹת שֶׁאִם מֵת נְכָסָיו לִפְלוֹנִי.
phrases.english: That it will be to stand and be, if he dies, his property to so-and-so.

phrases.aramaic: מַתָּנָה – כֹּל שֶׁכָּתוּב בּוֹ ״מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה״.
phrases.english: A gift – anything in which is written 'from today and after death'.

rashi.hebrew: דא תהא למיקם - שטר זה יהיה לקום ולהיות בכל הכתוב בו דדברי שכיב מרע ככתובין וכמסורין דמי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase דָּא תְּהֵא לְמֵיקַם means the document takes effect immediately upon the death of the שכיב מרע, whose words are treated as if already written and delivered.

rashi.hebrew: מתנה - מתנת בריא איזו היא כל שכתוב בו כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the term מַתָּנָה in this context refers specifically to a מתנת בריא, which is defined by the inclusion of the phrase מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה.

rashi.hebrew: מהיום ולאחר מיתה - גוף הקרקע קנוי לו מן היום ואין לו עוד רשות למוכרה וליתנה ולהורישה והפירות אינו אוכל כל ימי חייו אלא לאחר מיתה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in a מתנה מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה, the recipient acquires the principal of the land immediately, preventing the donor from selling or inheriting it, but the donor retains the right to consume the fruits until death.

steps.title: Challenge: Is acquisition only with 'from today and after death'?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the implication that a recipient only acquires property if the document explicitly states 'from today and after death'.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא infers a strict rule from the definition of מַתָּנָה and questions its validity. This is a challenge line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא reads the definition of מַתָּנָה as exclusive: only with the phrase 'מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה' does the recipient acquire, and without it, they do not. This seems to contradict the general understanding that a gift can be effective immediately or through other means.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the apparent exclusivity of the definition.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is not asking a question but attacking the implication of the text.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to clarify the scope of the ברייתא's definition.

phrases.aramaic: אַלְמָא אִי כְּתִיבָא ״מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה״ הוּא דְּקָנֵי,
phrases.english: Apparently, only if it is written: 'From today and after death' he acquires,
and if not –
he does not acquire?!

rashi.hebrew: ואי לא לא קני - בתמיה כל שכן דאם יהיב ליה גוף ופירות במעכשיו דקני טפי סתם מתנת שכיב מרע לא קניא לו אלא לאחר מיתה דמחמת מיתה הוא מצווה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge is rhetorical, arguing that if a gift lacking the phrase 'מֵהַיּוֹם וּלְאַחַר מִיתָה' is invalid, then certainly a standard gift from a שכיב מרע, which only takes effect after death, should be even more valid.

steps.title: Resolution: The ברייתא refers to a healthy person's gift resembling a deathbed gift
steps.stepSummary: Abaye resolves the challenge by explaining that the ברייתa is specifically discussing a gift from a healthy person that takes effect only after death, similar to a deathbed gift.
steps.whatsHappening: אביי clarifies the scope of the ברייתא's definition to resolve the apparent contradiction. This is a resolution line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: אביי explains that the ברייתא is not making a general statement about all gifts. It is specifically asking: 'What is a gift of a healthy person that is like a gift of a deathbed person, in that it is not acquired until after death?' The answer is: any gift written with 'from today and after death'. This limits the scope of the definition and resolves the challenge.
steps.whatToRemember: The definition applies specifically to מַתְנַת בָּרִיא that functions like מַתְנַת שְׁכִיב מְרַע.
steps.confusionAlert: The resolution hinges on the distinction between a healthy person and a deathbed person.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is fundamental to the laws of gifts and wills.

phrases.english: Abaye said, thus he says:

phrases.aramaic: אֵיזוֹ הִיא מַתְּנַת בָּרִיא שֶׁהִיא כְּמַתְּנַת שְׁכִיב מְרַע,
phrases.english: Which is a gift of a healthy person that is like a gift of a deathbed person,
that he does not acquire except after death,
anything that is written in it 'from today and after death'.

steps.title: Inference: If the giver says 'give', the finder must return the document
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara infers from the Mishnah that if the giver explicitly tells the finder to give the document, the finder must do so.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא draws a practical inference from the משנה's ruling about not returning found documents. This is a setup line for a new point of law.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה says not to return found wills or gifts. The גמרא infers that the reason is the absence of an explicit instruction to give. Therefore, if the giver does say 'תְּנוּ', the finder is obligated to return the document. This sets up a new point of law to be tested.
steps.whatToRemember: The inference is based on the absence of the word 'תְּנוּ' in the משנה.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a new inference, not a continuation of the previous discussion.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a condition under which a found document must be returned.

phrases.english: The reason is that he did not say 'Give'.
But he said 'Give' — they give.

rashi.hebrew: הא אמר תנו נותנין - אמתני' פריך דתלי טעמא בשאני אומר נמלך עליהם שלא לתנן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's inference (הא אמר תנו נותנין) is a direct challenge (פריך) to the Mishnah, which attributes the prohibition to the giver's change of mind (נמלך עליהם שלא לתנן), whereas the Gemara focuses on the absence of the explicit command 'תנו'.

steps.title: Challenge: A ברייתא says not to return even if both agree
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the inference by citing a ברייתא that states a found document should not be returned even if both the giver and recipient agree it is valid.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a contradictory source to test the inference from the משנה. This is a challenge line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא cites a ברייתא stating that if one finds wills, deeds of repayment, or gifts, even if both parties agree that the document is valid, the finder should not return it to either party. This directly contradicts the inference that if the giver says 'give', the finder must return it.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on a ברייתא that overrides the giver's instruction.
steps.confusionAlert: The challenge is not about the definition of the documents but about the ruling on returning them.
steps.whyThisMatters: This highlights a conflict between the משנה's implication and a ברייתא's explicit ruling.

phrases.english: And we raise them: Wills, deeds of designated repayment, and gifts,
Even though both of them admit –

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה!
phrases.english: He should not return to this one and not to that one!

rashi.hebrew: אפותיקאות - שעשה לו שדה אפותיקי למלוה על פה שהיתה לו עליו מלוה ישנה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אפותיקאות refers to documents designating a field as collateral for an existing oral debt.

rashi.hebrew: לא יחזיר - שמא כתבה לזה ולא מסר לו ולא זכה בקרקע וחזר וכתב לאחר ומסר לו השטר וזכה בו ובא לחזור בו מן השני ורוצה לתת לראשון וחפץ שימסרו לו שטר זה שיוציאנה בבית דין ונמצא זמן שטר של זה קודם לשטרו של שני ויוציאנה מידו בדין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the finder should not return the document because the giver might have written it for one person without transferring it, then wrote and transferred a similar document to another person, and now wishes to retract from the second person and use the first document to legally reclaim the collateral from the second person based on its earlier date.

steps.title: Resolution: Rabbi Abba bar Memel begins to resolve the contradiction
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Abba bar Memel begins to resolve the contradiction between the Mishnah's inference and the ברייתa.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a resolution to the contradiction. This is a setup line for a resolution that is not completed.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַבָּא בַּר מֵמֵל states 'לָא קַשְׁיָא', indicating that the two sources can be reconciled. The actual resolution is not provided in this sugya, as it ends here.
steps.whatToRemember: The resolution is introduced but not completed in this sugya.
steps.confusionAlert: The sugya ends abruptly after the introduction of the resolution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This indicates that the contradiction is resolvable, but the details are left for another discussion.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי אַבָּא בַּר מֶמֶל: לָא קַשְׁיָא,
phrases.english: Rabbi Abba bar Memel said: It is not difficult

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי אַבָּא בַּר מֶמֶל: לָא קַשְׁיָא
